# B21_KEY_MOMENTS

## Key Quotes

**"It's like the sink or swim of hardware around AI right now. They've all been sinking, so it'll be interesting to see some of them swim."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* Reacting to V's mention of the OpenAI / Jony Ive wearable. A clean framing of the AI hardware landscape — every device play (Humane Pin, Rabbit R1) has failed so far. Sets up the question of whether capital alone can overcome the pattern.

**"I have long maintained that you can artificially raise the quote unquote IQ of a system with appropriate systems engineering."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Discussing supervisor AI architectures and multi-agent orchestration. V articulating his core belief that disciplined architecture beats raw model capability — a cognitively disciplined but less intelligent system outperforms a brilliant but unreliable one. This is a reusable thesis statement for Careerspan's technical approach.

**"People are using AI to do big, which is the wrong direction."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* After observing that AI makes it trivially easy to generate volume, Alex distills the core product design failure of the current AI era into a single sentence. Directly validates Careerspan's "precision over volume" positioning.

**"The biggest thing AI does well is it handles a lot of data and it can distill it. But so many of these products, they're not really distilling it well. They're just still very verbose or time consuming."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* Critiquing Google's new Gmail AI features and the broader AI product landscape. Captures the gap between what the technology can do and what product teams are actually building — a theme that ran through the entire conversation.

**"We're going to give you precision candidates. Not a billion insights about a billion candidates. We're going to give you tight insights about a very curated pool of candidates. You're in the small mindset, which is what it should be."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* Unprompted characterization of Careerspan's value proposition. Significant because it comes from an outside product/UX expert affirming the strategy without coaching — a signal that the positioning lands clearly with sophisticated listeners.

**"It's like they rushed you in an airline cockpit and you're like, wow, there's a lot of switches in here. But this doesn't help me know how to fly a plane at all."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* Reacting to V's story about the interview prep tool that showed 10 speech metrics. A vivid analogy for the "data ≠ insight" trap that many AI products fall into — more dashboards, more metrics, zero actionable guidance.

**"I don't think raw prompting is going to exist soon. It's going to be people communicating in natural language and then there's being a ton of prompting behind the scenes and these middle layers that they've tuned and thrown guardrails on."**
— Alex Caveny
*Context:* Drawing an analogy from assembly language to Python to predict where AI interaction design is heading. Argues that the current prompting paradigm is as unsustainable as writing assembly — middle layers will abstract it away for real users.

**"The information you give your user to replace information they don't want to consume cannot suddenly be greater than the information they're consuming in the first place."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Articulating a first-principles UX rule for AI products. If the AI-generated summary is longer than the thing it's summarizing, you've failed. A reusable design principle for any AI product team.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"How do you reckon you could overcome the memory and context constraints to actually start prioritizing one thing versus the other?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* V probing Alex on the hardest technical problem in his notification prioritization idea — that prioritization requires external, dynamic context about what matters to the user. This is the same core challenge Careerspan faces with employer preference learning.

**"What does that interface look like — where if something gets through the notification filter that I didn't want, how can I say why?"**
— Alex Caveny
*Significance:* Framing the unsolved UX problem of preference feedback loops in AI systems. The question isn't whether AI can learn preferences — it's how users express them without becoming prompt engineers. Directly applicable to Careerspan's candidate feedback interface.

**"Part of the tendency towards giving a lot is knowing that the AI is going to have hits and misses — if we give them a lot, the hits are going to be in there somewhere. Versus if you give them a little, you don't want to give them one sentence and then the sentence is unhelpful."**
— Alex Caveny
*Significance:* Articulates the fundamental tension in AI product design: precision requires confidence, but current models aren't confident enough to be precise. This is the product design version of the accuracy-recall tradeoff and explains why most AI products default to verbosity as a hedging strategy.
